# Model Documentation — Consumer PD Application Scorecard

**Document type:** Model development & validation summary (SR 11-7 aligned)
**Model ID:** PD-APP-SCRCD-001
**Model type:** Probability of Default (PD) — application scorecard
**Owner:** `Tanmay Shrivastava` · **Status:** Demonstration / portfolio build
**Data:** Synthetic (see §2) · **Last updated:** generated by pipeline

This document follows the structure a Model Risk Management (MRM) function expects under
**SR 11-7**: model purpose, conceptual soundness, data, methodology, performance, ongoing
monitoring, governance and limitations.

---

## 1. Purpose & use

The model estimates the probability that a credit applicant becomes **90+ days past due within a
12-month performance window**. It is used at the point of application to **rank-order applicants by
risk** and to set an approval cut-off that balances portfolio growth, profitability and credit loss.
Outputs: (a) a PD, (b) a scaled credit score, and (c) adverse-action reason codes for declines.

**Intended use:** application underwriting for unsecured consumer lending (personal loans / cards).
**Out of scope:** behavioural/account-management scoring, collections prioritisation, pricing.

---

## 2. Data

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Source | **Synthetic**, generated by `src/data_generation.py` |
| Development sample | ~60,000 applications (2023 H1 vintage) |
| Out-of-time sample | ~20,000 applications (2024 H1 vintage) |
| Target | `default_flag` = 1 if 90+ DPD within 12 months, else 0 |
| Development bad rate | ~11.5% |
| Feature families | Application/demographic · Credit bureau · Behavioural/alternative |

The synthetic generator imposes economically realistic relationships (monotone risk in bureau score,
utilisation, delinquencies, DTI, inquiries; modest alternative-data lift) plus idiosyncratic noise so
that discrimination, calibration and stability behave like a real book. **No real consumer data is
used.** The methodology is data-agnostic and transfers to production data unchanged.

---

## 3. Methodology

### 3.1 Feature engineering — Weight of Evidence
Each predictor is binned (supervised, monotonic, ≥5% population per bin) and replaced by the
**Weight of Evidence** of its bin: `WOE = ln(%good / %bad)`. WOE linearises predictors against the
log-odds (the logistic assumption), tames outliers, and turns missing values into an informative bin.
**Information Value** ranks predictive power.

### 3.2 Feature selection (four gates)
1. **IV band** — retain usable predictive power; drop inert variables.
2. **Endogeneity / leakage** — exclude variables that are outcomes of the risk process. The offered
   **interest rate** is excluded despite high IV because it is priced off the predicted risk.
3. **Multicollinearity** — drop features with **VIF > 5** iteratively.
4. **Coefficient sign** — every WOE coefficient must be negative (higher WOE → lower PD); wrong-signed
   features are removed.

Final model: **9 characteristics** spanning all three data families (including an alternative-data
digital-engagement signal).

### 3.3 Model & scaling
**Logistic regression** on WOE inputs — chosen for transparency, monotonicity, additive structure and
clean mapping to points and reason codes. Scores are scaled to a points system:

```
factor = PDO / ln(2)                          # PDO = 20
offset = base_score − factor · ln(base_odds)  # 600 pts @ 50:1
points = −(β_i · WOE_i + intercept/n) · factor + offset/n
```

---

## 4. Performance

| Sample | N | Bad rate | KS | Gini | AUC | Brier |
|---|--:|--:|--:|--:|--:|--:|
| Train | 42,000 | 11.6% | 38.3 | 0.520 | 0.760 | 0.090 |
| Test (in-time) | 18,000 | 11.5% | 38.2 | 0.516 | 0.758 | 0.090 |
| Out-of-time | 20,000 | 20.3% | 39.4 | 0.528 | 0.764 | 0.135 |

- **Discrimination:** KS ~38, Gini ~0.52 — strong for an application scorecard.
- **No overfitting:** train ≈ test.
- **Rank-ordering:** monotone across all 10 score bands (37.5% → 2.3% bad rate; 16× spread).
- **Calibration:** predicted PD tracks observed default rate along the diagonal.

**Effective challenge:** XGBoost / Random Forest challengers improve Gini by < 0.01 while being far
less calibrated and not natively explainable — the interpretable champion is retained.

---

## 5. Ongoing monitoring

| Index | Measures | Thresholds | Current (dev → OOT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PSI** | Score-distribution shift | <0.10 stable · 0.10–0.25 investigate · >0.25 act | **0.24 (investigate)** |
| **CSI** | Per-characteristic shift | same | Utilisation **0.38**, bureau score 0.13 |

**Observed condition & action:** the OOT population has shifted materially riskier (PSI 0.24, bad rate
~doubled) **but discrimination held** (Gini stable). Diagnosis: the model still ranks correctly but is
**mis-calibrated** to the new population. **Recommended action: recalibrate the PD anchors and cut-off**
(not a full redevelopment), and investigate the booking-mix change driving utilisation drift.

---

## 6. Governance, fair lending & limitations

**Governance (SR 11-7):** conceptual soundness (transparent, signed, monotone model); outcomes
analysis (in-time + OOT); ongoing monitoring (PSI/CSI with action thresholds); effective challenge
(ML benchmark).

**Fair lending:** the additive points structure yields **adverse-action reason codes** directly from
each applicant's largest negative point contributions. Protected-class attributes are excluded by
construction; a production deployment would add disparate-impact testing on outcomes.

**Known limitations / next steps:**
- Synthetic data (by design) — production deployment requires real data and **reject inference** to
  correct for the truncation of declined applicants.
- Add segment-level scorecards (thin-file vs thick-file).
- Run the champion–challenger in production shadow mode.
- Quarterly recalibration cadence triggered by the PSI thresholds above.

---

*Prepared as a portfolio demonstration of credit-risk model development and governance.
Synthetic data only — see `src/data_generation.py`.*
